Phase One Architectural Review and Implementation Curriculum
Don Gunter — WodansEye Research Series — July 2026
Tentative architecture review for implementation-team discussion.

Review verdict
The Phase One package is healthy as a teaching and reference package. All five shipped tests compile and pass under C++20 with strict warnings enabled:
-Wall -Wextra -Wpedantic -Wconversion -WshadowThe package demonstrates several useful ideas correctly:

• one operational vocabulary centered on T;

• reference implementations before accelerated implementations;

• round-trip tests;

• bounded stream lookahead;

• late resolution through stable IDs;

• early symbol tables and macro expansion;

• visible junctions in a chain;

• a Terry-style climb from small tools toward language machinery.
However, the document currently places too much architectural weight on the first callable wrapper, the scalar/vector/matrix/network “rank ladder,” and a compile-time Chain that only works when every stage shares an assignable value type.
The production center is different.
The permanent center is a typed translation registry represented as a Boost Graph.
The vertices are canonical information or artifact types.
The edges state that a translation relation is available between two types.
The edge data carries cost, conditions, provenance, and a reference to one or more T implementations.
A planner chooses a path or plan through that graph.
Only then is an executable chain materialized.
This is the architectural correction required before the next implementation phase.
The project remains sound. The current code should be retained as tutorial scaffolding and a test laboratory, but Phase One should not be declared complete until the type registry, translation graph, path planner, and heterogeneous plan executor exist.

1. The central clarification: everything that does work is a T
The phrase “everything is a T” is useful if it is stated precisely.
A value is not a T.
A type is not a T.
A file is not a T.
A graph vertex is not a T.
A graph edge is not, by itself, a T.
A relation such as:
CSV_Table can_translate_to SQL_Insert_Scriptis not the execution. It is a declaration that an execution is available.
The work that realizes that relation is a T.
Therefore:
Every operation that reads, analyzes, transforms, generates, writes, compiles, links, schedules, validates, or executes is a T. Data and declared relations are the typed domains over which T operates.
Examples:
Path -> ByteStream
ByteStream -> BlockStream
BlockStream -> FileHeader
FileHeader -> FileDefinition
CSV_RowStream -> TableGraph
TableGraph -> SQL_Document
TemplateDocument -> ExpandedDocument
GrammarGraph -> DFA
SyntaxGraph -> TypedSyntaxGraph
TypedSyntaxGraph -> LLVM_Module
LLVM_Module -> JIT_Function
ArtifactSet -> BuildReportEvery arrow may be implemented by a T.
A composite route through several arrows is also exposed as one T after planning and assembly.
This retains the one-base-abstraction principle without confusing operations with data structures.

2. The three graphs that must not be conflated
The current document uses “chain,” “network,” and “graph” closely enough that the implementation team may accidentally merge different responsibilities. Phase One should teach three distinct graphs.
2.1 The type and translation graph
This is the permanent registry.
Vertices
Vertices identify canonical information domains:
RawBytes
UTF8Text
DotTokens
DotCST
ETreeGraph
GrammarGraph
DFA
CalculatorAST
ToyCAST
PCodeModule
LLVMModule
NativeFunction
CSVRows<CustomerV2>
SQLInsertDocument<PostgreSQL>
MarkdownDocument
PDFDocumentA type identity must be stable beyond one C++ process. std::type_index is useful inside a process but is not sufficient as the durable identity for generated files, plugins, agents, serialized plans, or cross-language tools.
A canonical TypeId should include or resolve:
namespace
name
version
schema
representation
encoding
ownership or access formEdges
A directed edge means:
a translation capability exists from source type to target typeThe edge is a relation.
Its attached descriptor references the T implementation or a factory that can construct one.
Type A
-- TranslationRelation / descriptor -->
Type BThe descriptor may carry:
translation_id
source_type
target_type
role
cardinality
effect
activation
requirements
lossiness
determinism
streaming capability
batch capability
working-memory estimate
startup cost
per-item cost
energy estimate
quality or precision class
provenance
implementation handle or factorySeveral parallel edges may connect the same two types:
HeronInput -> TriangleAreaedge 1: naive double
edge 2: Kahan-stable double
edge 3: scaled stable double
edge 4: exact squared area
edge 5: interval-certified areaThe graph declares options. It does not force one universal path.
2.2 The planned translation graph
A request supplies:
source type
target type
policy
available resources
required guaranteesThe planner chooses a path or a plan.
A simple request may produce a linear path:
UTF8Text
-> DotTokens
-> DotCST
-> ETreeGraphA larger request may produce a DAG:
-> C++ source
ETreeGraph -> ModelIR -> Markdown docs
-> DOT diagram
-> LLVM IRThe plan graph contains selected translations, required adapters, common subexpressions, buffers, joins, and ordering constraints.
It is temporary and request-specific.
2.3 The build and artifact dependency graph
The build graph answers another question:
Which artifacts depend on which other artifacts, and which must be regenerated?
Its vertices are files, in-memory artifacts, generated documents, object files, test results, and targets.
Its edges are dependency relations, not translation choices.
A build task may consult the translation graph to determine how to regenerate an artifact, but the build graph determines whether and when that work is required.
These graphs cooperate:
Translation graph:
What conversions are possible?Plan graph:
Which conversions should this request use?Build graph:
Which resulting artifacts are stale or required?This separation is essential.

3. Boost Graph is the graph floor
The project should not write its own general graph-algorithm framework before it has used Boost Graph.
Boost Graph already provides:

• graph concepts;

• adjacency_list;

• bundled vertex and edge properties;

• external property maps;

• visitors;

• filtered graph views;

• Dijkstra shortest paths;

• all-pairs algorithms;

• topological sorting;

• strongly connected components;

• minimum spanning trees;

• graph traversal algorithms.
E-tree should initially supply the domain model and property types around those facilities.
A tentative graph form is:
Vertex property:
TypeDescriptorEdge property:
TranslationDescriptorThe storage choice may begin with boost::adjacency_list.
Later, a frozen E-tree structure may adapt to Boost Graph concepts when measurement proves that custom storage is useful. The algorithmic interface remains Boost-compatible.
This preserves the inside-out rule:
Borrow the proven graph mathematics first. Optimize representation after the semantics and conformance tests are stable.

4. The edge contains a T reference, but the edge is not the T
The author’s latest formulation is the correct one:
Relations are edges connecting two information flows. The weight and edge data contain the translation.
The precise interpretation should be:
Edge semantics:
“A translation may be performed from A to B.”Edge property:
descriptor, cost, restrictions, and T handle/factoryT:
the executable operation selected when a plan materializes the edgeThis small distinction gives the architecture room to support:

• unavailable implementations;

• plugin-loaded implementations;

• generated implementations;

• interpreted implementations;

• JIT implementations;

• several implementations for one relation;

• remote agent tools;

• exact and approximate variants;

• platform-specific variants.
The registry should not store a raw callable as its only truth.
It should store a stable TranslationId and a descriptor that can resolve or construct the callable in the current environment.

5. The translation dictionary and its indexes
The “static map of translations indexed by input and output type” should be implemented as two cooperating structures.
5.1 Direct pair index
A direct index answers quickly:
What registered translations map A directly to B?Conceptually:
(SourceTypeId, TargetTypeId) -> [TranslationId...]This is useful for:

• direct conversion;

• overload selection;

• diagnostics;

• fluent DSL completion;

• agent tool discovery;

• generated registry code.
5.2 Boost translation graph
The graph supports multi-stage discovery:
A -> B -> C -> DIt also supports filtering edges according to policy.
Examples:
allow only lossless translations
allow only deterministic translations
require streaming
forbid filesystem effects
prefer already-JIT-compiled translations
prefer exact arithmetic
prefer low startup latency
prefer low total energyThe direct index and graph are views over the same registry records.
They must not become independent sources of truth.
5.3 Static and dynamic registries
Two registry layers are likely required.
Static registry
Generated or compiled with the program:

• built-in readers;

• built-in writers;

• common adapters;

• core syntax tools;

• stable type definitions;

• trusted LLVM paths.
Dynamic registry
Extended during execution:

• plugins;

• project-defined DSL tools;

• agent tools;

• generated translations;

• JIT functions;

• remote services;

• newly loaded schemas.
A registry generation number should change whenever capabilities change, so cached plans can be invalidated safely.

6. Dijkstra is the first planner, not the final planner
For a simple one-source, one-target, sequential translation request, Dijkstra’s algorithm is the correct first tool.
Each edge has a nonnegative cost.
The planner finds the least-cost path from source type to target type.
Boost Graph allows the distance type, comparison operation, combination operation, and property maps to be customized. Therefore “cost” need not be only an integer instruction count.
A first cost may be a policy-selected scalar:
cost =
α * startup
+ β * runtime
+ γ * memory
+ δ * energy
+ ε * information_lossA better later form may use lexicographic or constrained cost:
1. reject any path that violates required guarantees
2. minimize information loss
3. minimize total runtime
4. minimize startup
5. minimize energyThe policy converts the richer descriptor into the distance type used by the selected graph search.
6.1 What ordinary Dijkstra handles well

• one source type;

• one target type;

• sequential directed edges;

• nonnegative edge costs;

• alternative implementations;

• filtered capability graphs.
6.2 What ordinary Dijkstra does not solve alone
A mux may require two or more inputs before one translation can execute.
A generator may produce several targets.
A build may request several artifacts that share a common prefix.
Parallel synthesis may involve forks and joins.
These are not ordinary single-path problems.
The first implementation may represent some of them using synthetic vertices:
InputA
InputB
\ /
JoinRequirementNode
|
T
|
OutputBut the architecture should acknowledge that the general planner is an AND/OR, hypergraph, or state-space planner.
A sensible growth path is:
Phase A: Dijkstra for linear one-input paths
Phase B: shortest-path tree for several targets
Phase C: plan DAG with common-prefix reuse
Phase D: explicit mux/join requirements
Phase E: multi-objective and resource-constrained planningThe document should not imply that one Dijkstra call automatically schedules every parallel analysis and synthesis flow.

7. A selected path becomes a fast chain
The translation registry is dynamic and descriptive.
The hot execution path should not repeatedly traverse the registry.
Planning should produce a materialized executable plan.
registry lookup and graph
